title: The Great Anastasia Mystery Solved
story: A woman claimed to be the long-lost Russian duchess Anastasia Romanov, sparking a mystery that captivated the world. For nearly three decades, Anna Anderson insisted she had survived her family's execution. But on February 28, 1967, a West German court finally settled the debate. They ruled that Anderson couldn't prove she was really Anastasia, ending one of history's most famous identity puzzles.

title: The Gritty Discovery of Carborundum
story: In a Pennsylvania lab, Edward Acheson made a sparkling breakthrough in 1893. While experimenting with electricity and clay, he accidentally created a super-hard crystal. Acheson named his shiny new substance 'carborundum.' This gritty material, now known as silicon carbide, became a game-changer for sharpening and polishing tools. On February 28, Acheson secured a patent for his accidental invention.
activity_question: What did Edward Acheson name his newly discovered substance?
bonus_fact: Carborundum is nearly as hard as diamonds, making it excellent for industrial grinding and polishing.

title: Shaky Ground in Washington State
story: The ground rumbled and buildings swayed as a powerful earthquake struck Washington State in 2001. On February 28, the Nisqually Earthquake shook the Seattle, Tacoma, and Olympia areas. Measuring 6.8 on the Richter scale, it was one of the strongest quakes to hit the region in years. Despite its strength, the earthquake's deep location helped limit damage. Communities came together to repair and rebuild, learning valuable lessons about earthquake preparedness.
activity_question: What was the strength of the Nisqually Earthquake on the Richter scale?
follow_up_question: Why is it important for people in earthquake-prone areas to be prepared?

story: Imagine stepping onto a lush tropical shore after months at sea! That's what Charles Darwin experienced in 1832. title: Rhythm Master Born in Gold Coast
story: Kwasi Dzidzornu, better known as Rocky Dijon, was born on February 28, 1932, in the British Gold Coast colony, now Ghana. This talented percussionist would go on to make waves in the music world, playing with legendary artists like The Rolling Stones, Taj Mahal, and Stevie Wonder. His rhythmic skills added a unique flavor to rock and R&B, bridging African beats with Western music.

title: The Mysterious Case of the Ghost Word

The ghost word vanished in later editions, leaving behind a fascinating tale of dictionary detective work.

title: The Beatles' Favorite Club Shuts Its Doors

This underground hangout was famous as the place where The Beatles played nearly 300 shows early in their career. title: Mary Lyon: Champion of Women's Education
story: Mary Lyon blazed a trail for women's education. Born near Buckland, Massachusetts in 1797, she grew up to become a passionate teacher and activist. Lyon believed girls deserved the same educational opportunities as boys. Her dream came true when she founded Mount Holyoke Female Seminary, serving as its first principal. On February 28, her birthday, we remember this pioneer who opened doors for countless women to learn and grow.

story: Brave women workers made history in 1909. They stood up for fair treatment at their jobs, inspiring the first National Woman's Day on February 28. This special day honored garment workers who had protested poor working conditions in New York the year before. Their courage sparked a movement for women's rights that still echoes today.

title: A Dazzling Tower Rises in San Francisco
story: Sparkling gems and shimmering lights! In 1914, workers began building the magnificent Tower of Jewels for the San Francisco Exposition. This towering structure would soon become the centerpiece of a grand fair, catching sunlight and dazzling visitors with thousands of colorful glass 'jewels' hanging from its walls. On February 28, the first stones were laid, starting an exciting journey to create this unforgettable wonder.
activity_question: What was the Tower of Jewels built for?
bonus_fact: The Tower of Jewels was covered in over 100,000 cut glass 'jewels' that sparkled in the sunlight.

title: Rugby's Game-Changing Move
story: Touchdown! In 1931, Canadian Rugby Union players got a new way to score. On February 28, they adopted the forward pass. This exciting change let players throw the ball ahead, making the game faster and more thrilling. It opened up new strategies and changed how teams played. The forward pass soon became a key part of Canadian football.

title: Pat Verbeek's Hockey Hat Trick Plus One
story: Pucks flew as Pat Verbeek lit up the ice rink in 1988. On February 28, he became the first New Jersey Devils player to score four goals in a single NHL game. Fans cheered as Verbeek's lightning-fast shots found the net again and again. His amazing performance helped the team and showed why hockey is such an exciting sport to watch.
activity_question: What did Pat Verbeek achieve for the New Jersey Devils?

He broke the record for most assists
activity_answer: He became the first to score four goals in a game

title: Poet Sparks Anti-Nuclear Movement
story: A famous poet stood up against nuclear weapons in 1989. Olzhas Suleimenov started the Nevada-Semipalatinsk movement in Kazakhstan on February 28. He wanted to shut down a big nuclear testing site. His brave actions inspired many people to join the cause. Their efforts paid off when the site closed just two years later.
activity_question: What was the name of the anti-nuclear movement started by Olzhas Suleimenov?
follow_up_question: Why do you think it's important for people to speak up about things they believe in?
